text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY SURVEILLANCE VISIT
Renata Okonkwo | DOB: [on file] | MRN: [on file]
Attending: [Oncologist] | Fellow/Resident: [on file]
Visit Type: Established Patient, Surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Okonkwo is a 40-year-old Nigerian-American premenopausal woman (now surgically menopausal) with a history of Stage IIB triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast (ypT1b ypN1 M0 following neoadjuvant therapy, RCB class II), BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant carrier, who presents today for her first scheduled oncology surveillance visit approximately 6 months after completing all planned cancer-directed therapy. She was referred also to the menopause clinic; today's visit focuses on surveillance, late effects management, and survivorship planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo completed an extensive treatment course for her triple-negative breast cancer, which was diagnosed approximately 18 months ago when she discovered a left breast mass. Her treatment course included:

1. Neoadjuvant Keytruda 200 mg IV q3 weeks combined with dose-dense Adriamycin/cyclo (AC) every 2 weeks x 4 cycles, followed by weekly PTX 80 mg/m2 (dose-reduced to 64 mg/m2 after Grade 2 neuropathy) x 12 weeks plus Keytruda q3 weeks x 4 doses -- all administered per KEYNOTE-522 protocol.
2. Bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy with left axillary lymph node dissection (14 nodes removed, 2 positive), plus right prophylactic mastectomy, with immediate bilateral tissue expander placement.
3. Adjuvant Xeloda (dose-reduced from 1710 mg BID to 1250 mg BID after Grade 2-3 hand-foot syndrome) for 8 cycles, plus adjuvant pembro to complete 1 year total (17 doses total across neoadjuvant and adjuvant phases).
4. Stage 2 breast reconstruction with exchange of tissue expanders for silicone implants.
5. Risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) via laparoscopy approximately 2 months ago, given confirmed BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant (c.5074del, p.Cys1692ValfsTer17). Pathology from RRSO was entirely benign -- no STIC lesions, no malignancy.

Notable treatment-related complications included: pembro-induced thyroiditis (initially thyrotoxic, then hypothyroid, now managed on levothyroxine), Grade 2-3 hand-foot syndrome from Xeloda (resolved after dose reduction), peripheral neuropathy (currently residual Grade 1), and Grade 2 immune-related pneumonitis with the final (17th) dose of Keytruda, which was managed with prednisone 1 mg/kg and has since fully resolved.

The patient presents today reporting several ongoing post-treatment concerns:

SURGICAL MENOPAUSE SYMPTOMS: Since the RRSO performed approximately 2 months ago, she has developed moderate-to-severe surgical menopause symptoms. She describes 6 to 8 hot flashes per day, including several at night that are disrupting her sleep. She reports profuse night sweats requiring change of nightclothes approximately 2-3 times per week. She also describes mood lability with episodes of irritability and sadness, which she attributes in part to hormonal changes and in part to the psychological weight of her cancer history. She has noticed vaginal dryness and mild dyspareunia. She was referred to the menopause clinic and has been prescribed venlafaxine, which she reports has just been initiated within the last week -- too early to assess benefit. She specifically asked about hormonal options; she has been counseled that systemic hormone replacement therapy is generally not recommended given her TNBC history, and she accepts this.

NEUROPATHY: She reports bilateral fingertip tingling, slightly worse on the right than the left. She estimates it is about 30-40% improved since completing pacli. She is able to type, button clothing, and write without significant difficulty. No new numbness in the feet, though she still notices mild sensitivity when walking barefoot on cold floors.

FATIGUE: Moderate fatigue remains, rated 4-5/10. She returned to work as a high school biology teacher on a part-time basis last month and is planning to return full-time next semester. She denies dyspnea at rest. She can climb a flight of stairs without becoming short of breath.

PSYCHOSOCIAL: She reports significant anxiety regarding recurrence, which she rates as her primary daily stressor. She is engaged with oncology social work and has been attending a cancer survivor support group for young women. She describes a strong support system with her partner and parents. She has two children (ages 8 and 11) and has been navigating how and when to discuss BRCA1 implications with them and with her siblings.

No new or concerning symptoms: She denies new lumps, masses, or skin changes over the bilateral reconstructed breast areas or chest wall. She denies bone pain, back pain, or new headaches. No shortness of breath or pleuritic chest pain (pneumonitis resolved). No abdominal pain, jaundice, or unintentional weight loss. No dysuria, pelvic pain, or new adnexal symptoms post-RRSO. She has not noticed any new lymphadenopathy.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Moderate fatigue, 4-5/10. No fever. No chills. No unintentional weight loss (weight has been stable at 67 kg). Mild decreased appetite.
Head/ENT: No headaches, visual changes, or hearing changes.
Cardiopulmonary: Dyspnea on exertion with vigorous activity only (improved from prior). No shortness of breath at rest. No cough (resolved). No chest pain or palpitations (atenolol was previously prescribed for thyrotoxic phase; this has since been discontinued).
Gastrointestinal: No nausea. Mild constipation (attributed to surgical menopause and possible levothyroxine underdosing being evaluated). No diarrhea. No blood in stool.
Musculoskeletal: Bilateral joint aches, mild, attributed to post-menopausal state. No focal bone pain.
Neurologic: Bilateral fingertip paresthesias (Grade 1, slowly improving). No new focal weakness. No headaches.
Genitourinary: Vaginal dryness and mild dyspareunia as above. No urinary incontinence. Post-RRSO, no menstrual periods (expected).
Skin: Bilateral mastectomy scars well-healed. Mild residual hand and foot skin dryness, sequela of prior hand-foot syndrome, no active blistering or erythema. Alopecia: hair has regrown to approximately 3 cm, diffuse, slightly different texture than baseline per patient.
Endocrine: Surgical menopause symptoms as above. No symptoms of adrenal insufficiency.
Psychiatric: Anxiety regarding recurrence. Mood lability (attributed to surgical menopause). Engaged with support services.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 112/70 mmHg, heart rate 66 bpm (regular), temperature 37.0 C, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 67 kg, BSA 1.71 m2.

General: Ms. Okonkwo is a well-appearing young woman in no acute distress. She is alert, interactive, and engaged. Hair regrowth noted -- short, curly, approximately 3 cm length. She appears slightly fatigued but otherwise in excellent condition.

Head and Neck: No cervical, supraclavicular, or infraclavicular lymphadenopathy appreciated on palpation bilaterally. Thyroid is non-tender and non-enlarged on palpation. No lymphadenopathy in the anterior or posterior cervical chains.

Chest/Breast: Bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy sites with silicone implant reconstruction in place bilaterally. Scars are well-healed, non-erythematous, non-tender. No palpable masses, nodules, or firmness over the chest wall bilaterally that would be concerning for locoregional recurrence. The implants are soft with normal contour bilaterally. No skin dimpling, peau d'orange changes, or nipple abnormalities. The bilateral axillae are soft with no palpable lymphadenopathy. Post-ALND changes on the left (mild firmness) -- no new nodularity.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezes, or decreased breath sounds. Good air entry throughout. (Gratifying, given recent history of immune pneumonitis.)

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable masses. RRSO laparoscopic port sites well-healed.

Extremities: No pitting edema. Mild trace left arm lymphedema noted (increase in forearm circumference approximately 1 cm compared to the right) -- stable from prior, has been wearing compression sleeve. Sensation to light touch slightly diminished in bilateral fingertips and distal toes.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor deficits. Bilateral distal sensory deficits (fingertips and toes) as noted above, consistent with residual chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN), Grade 1 by CTCAE.

Psychiatric: Appropriate affect, mildly anxious. Thought process linear and logical. Good insight.

---

RELEVANT LABORATORY RESULTS

Drawn at today's visit (fasting):

CBC:
  - WBC 6.1 x 10^9/L (normal)
  - ANC 3,800/uL (normal)
  - Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL (mildly low; iron studies pending given fatigue)
  - Platelets 241 x 10^9/L (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride 102 mEq/L, CO2 25 mEq/L
  - BUN 14 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
  - Glucose 88 mg/dL (fasting)
  - Total protein 7.1 g/dL, Albumin 4.2 g/dL
  - Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, Direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL
  - ALT 22 U/L, AST 20 U/L (normal -- previously elevated Grade 1 during cape, now resolved)
  - Alkaline phosphatase 74 U/L (normal)

Thyroid Function:
  - TSH 2.4 mIU/L (within normal range; stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily)
  - Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)

Hormone Panel (post-RRSO):
  - FSH 62 IU/L (markedly elevated, consistent with post-oophorectomy state)
  - Estradiol <10 pg/mL (post-menopausal range, as expected)
  - LH 58 IU/L (elevated)
  - Note: No hormonal therapy to be initiated given TNBC history

Iron Studies (added given mild anemia/fatigue):
  - Serum iron: 58 mcg/dL (low-normal)
  - TIBC: 390 mcg/dL (elevated)
  - Transferrin saturation: 15% (mildly low)
  - Ferritin: 11 ng/mL (low)
  - Impression: Mild iron deficiency anemia, likely dietary in setting of decreased appetite and prior chemotherapy; will start oral iron supplementation

Note: Per ASCO guidelines, CA 27.29 and CA 15-3 tumor markers are NOT routinely monitored in TNBC surveillance and were not drawn.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (obtained prior to today's visit, read by radiology):
No evidence of locoregional recurrence or distant metastatic disease. Bilateral silicone implants in place. No pulmonary nodules. Prior ground-glass opacities from immune-related pneumonitis are completely resolved -- lungs are now clear. Liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys are unremarkable. No hepatic lesions. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the mediastinum, abdomen, or pelvis. Post-surgical changes from laparoscopic RRSO noted in the pelvis. No adnexal masses, ascites, or peritoneal deposits.

Impression: No evidence of recurrent or metastatic breast cancer.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Okonkwo is a 40-year-old woman with Stage IIB TNBC, BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant carrier, who has completed all planned curative-intent therapy approximately 6 months ago. She is currently with no evidence of disease by examination and surveillance imaging. She has multiple ongoing late effects and quality-of-life concerns that require active management.

1. SURVEILLANCE -- NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE
Surveillance CT today shows no evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease. This is reassuring. I reviewed the scans with her and she is relieved. She continues to carry meaningful risk for recurrence given TNBC histology, non-pCR status (RCB class II, ypT1b ypN1), and BRCA1 carrier status, with the highest recurrence risk in the first 2-3 years after diagnosis. We discussed this in plain language -- I told her that while the scans look great right now, it is important that she continue close follow-up and promptly report any new symptoms.
  - Continue oncology surveillance visits every 3 months for the first 2 years post-treatment, then every 6 months in years 3-5.
  - Annual chest wall/skin surveillance MRI of bilateral reconstructed breasts (next due at 12 months post-surgery, approximately 6 months from now) -- I re-emphasized this is NOT the same as a mammogram, and she understands this is the appropriate modality given bilateral mastectomy.
  - Next surveillance CT in approximately 12 months (or sooner if symptoms arise).
  - Per ASCO guidelines, routine tumor markers (CA 27.29, CA 15-3, CEA) are NOT recommended for TNBC surveillance; imaging is symptom-driven and per schedule.

2. SURGICAL MENOPAUSE / RRSO-RELATED SYMPTOMS
She is experiencing significant hot flashes, night sweats, sleep disruption, vaginal dryness, and mood changes following RRSO 2 months ago. Systemic HRT is not recommended in this context given breast cancer history (TNBC). We discussed non-hormonal options:
  - Venlafaxine 75 mg daily (recently initiated by menopause clinic) -- will reassess benefit at next visit. May uptitrate to 150 mg if partial response.
  - Vaginal moisturizers (Replens or similar) for local vaginal dryness -- recommended daily application. Low-dose vaginal estradiol cream was discussed; given TNBC (not hormone-receptor positive), the absolute risk is likely very low, but we agreed to defer this discussion to her gynecologic oncologist given the BRCA1 context.
  - Referred to integrative oncology for acupuncture and mind-body interventions for hot flashes.
  - Bone health counseling: with surgical menopause at age 40, she is at increased risk for accelerated bone loss. Recommended weight-bearing exercise, calcium supplementation (1000-1200 mg/day from dietary and supplemental sources), and vitamin D3 1000 IU daily. Baseline DXA scan ordered.
  - Cardiovascular risk counseling: surgical menopause at a young age increases long-term cardiovascular risk; encouraged regular aerobic exercise, heart-healthy diet, and ongoing primary care follow-up for lipids and blood pressure.

3. CHEMOTHERAPY-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (CIPN)
Residual Grade 1 CIPN in bilateral fingertips and toes, slowly improving over the past several months. Reassured her that Grade 1 neuropathy from pacli often continues to improve over 12-24 months post-completion of therapy, though some degree may be permanent.
  - No pharmacologic treatment indicated for Grade 1 CIPN.
  - Recommend physical therapy evaluation if symptoms plateau or worsen.
  - Fall precautions counseled given mild toe numbness.
  - No dose modifications to any current medications needed.

4. IRON DEFICIENCY ANEMIA / FATIGUE
Mild iron deficiency anemia identified on today's labs (ferritin 11 ng/mL, transferrin saturation 15%, Hgb 12.4 g/dL) in the setting of decreased appetite, dietary insufficiency, and residual post-chemotherapy effects. This likely explains a portion of her fatigue.
  - Initiate ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO daily with vitamin C to enhance absorption.
  - Dietary counseling regarding iron-rich foods.
  - Repeat CBC and iron studies in 6-8 weeks.
  - Anemia workup otherwise deferred -- no evidence of hemolysis or nutritional B12/folate deficiency on today's labs.

5. IMMUNE-RELATED HYPOTHYROIDISM (Keytruda-INDUCED)
Well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH 2.4 mIU/L at goal. Free T4 normal.
  - Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily.
  - Recheck TSH in 6 months.
  - She has been counseled that Keytruda-induced hypothyroidism is typically permanent, requiring lifelong thyroid hormone replacement.

6. IMMUNE-RELATED PNEUMONITIS -- RESOLVED
Grade 2 pneumonitis from final dose of pembro was treated with high-dose prednisone and has fully resolved. Chest CT today confirms complete radiographic clearance. Prednisone taper was completed. SpO2 98% on room air.
  - No further intervention required.
  - Keytruda is permanently discontinued (was the final planned dose; patient received full 17 doses / 1 year total).

7. LYMPHEDEMA SURVEILLANCE
Mild left arm lymphedema (post-ALND), trace, stable. Circumferential measurement 1 cm difference forearm.
  - Continue compression sleeve as directed.
  - Continue lymphedema therapy program.
  - Reinforce precautions: avoid blood draws, IV access, and blood pressure measurement in the left arm.

8. BRCA1 CARRIER STATUS AND FAMILY IMPLICATIONS
Patient confirmed BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant (c.5074del). She has completed RRSO, which significantly reduces her ovarian cancer risk. She has two children, ages 8 and 11. She has been working with genetic counseling on how to communicate BRCA1 implications to her children (generally recommended genetic testing at ages 18-25 for BRCA1/2) and to her siblings and other first-degree relatives.
  - Continue engagement with genetic counseling.
  - Encouraged discussion with her siblings, particularly any sisters and maternal-lineage family members who should be offered testing.
  - Annual chest wall/skin MRI for breast cancer surveillance ongoing.
  - No further cancer prevention surgical interventions indicated at this time (RRSO completed; bilateral mastectomy completed).

9. PSYCHOSOCIAL WELLBEING
Ms. Okonkwo continues to experience significant recurrence anxiety and adjustment to her new post-cancer identity and post-RRSO hormonal state. She is engaged with oncology social work, participating in a young women's breast cancer support group, and seeing a therapist individually.
  - Strongly encouraged continued engagement with psychological support.
  - Validated the difficulty of navigating this diagnosis at age 39-40, including fertility implications (embryos banked prior to chemotherapy), surgical menopause, BRCA1 carrier status, and concerns about impact on her children.
  - Discussed recurrence anxiety management -- reviewed that today's scan is clear and that she is doing everything right.

10. MEDICATIONS (CURRENT)
  - Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (immune-related hypothyroidism)
  - Venlafaxine 75 mg PO daily (initiated by menopause clinic for hot flashes/mood)
  - New: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO daily (iron deficiency anemia)
  - New: Calcium carbonate 600 mg + Vitamin D3 1000 IU PO daily (bone health, post-RRSO)
  - PRN: Loperamide (held in reserve for diarrhea, not currently needed)
  - Vitamin C 500 mg PO daily with iron (to enhance absorption)
  - All prior cancer-directed therapies have been completed and discontinued.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Follow-up: Oncology visit in 3 months. Menopause clinic in 4 weeks. DXA scan ordered. Repeat labs in 6-8 weeks. Annual MRI of bilateral reconstructed breasts due in approximately 6 months.

Time spent with patient: 55 minutes, including discussion of survivorship concerns, review of imaging, examination, and care coordination.

Electronic signature: [Attending Oncologist, MD]
[Fellow/Resident, MD]
Division of Medical Oncology
